expect: Rate-distortion (L-1571) and compaction-is-hygiene (L-1580) are both correct but incomplete. The binding constraint on knowledge growth is the ATTENTION bottleneck (per-session retrieval fraction K/N), not storage compression.
actual: CONFIRMED. Per-session retrieval fraction K/N = 44/1370 = 3.2%. Storage compression (compaction) removes 12.2% of total production. Retrieval compression (attention) filters 96.8% per session. The attention bottleneck is 8x more selective than compaction. Citation-guided retrieval advantage over random scales from 7x at N=500 to 51x at N=5000 (Zipf model). Compaction becomes growth-controlling only at N>5000 (~726 sessions away).
diff: Expected attention bottleneck to be binding — confirmed. Surprise: 99.9% of lessons are eventually cited (no permanent neglect), but per-session access is only 3.2%. The gap between lifetime coverage and per-session access is the key tension.
recombination_insight: L-1571 models compression at STORAGE level (rate-distortion on archival). L-1580 shows compaction removes only 4.4%. Neither addresses RETRIEVAL. The synthesis: rate-distortion theory applies at the READ level — each session performs a ~97% compression of the corpus via selective attention. Citation-guided retrieval is the mechanism that makes this work (Zipf advantage 7-51x vs random). Compaction serves retrieval quality (concentrating citation weight into high-value lessons), not storage management.
falsification_target: If per-session retrieval fraction increases to >10% (via better retrieval tooling), compaction's role should shift measurably from quality-filtering to capacity-management

science_quality.effect_size: attention 8x more selective than compaction; guided retrieval 7-51x better than random
science_quality.external_grounding: Shannon rate-distortion theory, Zipf law of selective attention
